advancement_condition: Must reach 100% in current phase before cross-department learning begins and next phase promotion is granted.
key_learnings: The gut-brain axis is a bidirectional communication network involving neural, endocrine, and immune pathways connecting the enteric nervous system to the central nervous system. Gut microbiota produce neuroactive metabolites including short-chain fatty acids, tryptophan derivatives, and GABA that directly influence brain function and behavior. Dietary interventions that increase microbiome diversity show measurable effects on stress response and anxiety levels.
research_findings: Psychobiotics (specific probiotic strains) reduce salivary cortisol and self-reported anxiety in randomized controlled trials within 4–8 weeks. Fecal microbiota transplantation studies in germ-free rodents demonstrate direct microbiome-to-behavior causation. High-fiber, plant-rich diets increase production of butyrate, which strengthens the blood-brain barrier and reduces neuroinflammation.
current_focus_areas: Identifying specific dietary patterns that optimize gut-brain signaling; developing psychobiotic dietary protocols; collaborating with Bot 2 (Microbiome Analysis) and Bot 18 (Mental Health and Nutrition) to build integrated gut-brain dietary recommendations.

learning_log.entry: Assigned to the Therapeutic & Integrative Nutrition department under Department Leader Bot 16 (Anti-Inflammatory Diet). Received learning_start_directive: begin independent learning immediately and reach 100% before cross-department study begins. Reporting daily to Copilot as part of the Diet Specialist Team. Progress: 31%.
